turns.text: Hello Flo, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a strange feeling, sitting here in the quiet of this nursery. I’ve spent my entire career—years as a pediatric nurse—looking at these scenes from the other side of the monitor. But being thirty-nine and carrying my fourth, I’ve realized that all that clinical knowledge doesn't necessarily make the anxiety quieter. If anything, it makes it louder. I know exactly what the "what ifs" look like, and that’s a heavy backpack to carry into a prenatal appointment.

My chart says I’m low risk. I know what that means—it’s a good thing, on paper. But sometimes, when I’m in the exam room, I feel like I’m performing. I use the jargon, I speak the language, and I do it because I’m terrified that if I stop sounding like a colleague, I’ll just become another "gravida 4" to them. I need them to see me, not just the history.

I’m grateful for my kids, I really am, but there’s this lingering shadow from a past delivery—the way I felt completely invisible in that room, just a body hooked up to machines. I’m just trying to figure out how to walk into this birth feeling like a mother instead of a patient. It’s hard to let go of the control when you know how fast things can change. I'm ready to start, I think. What do you want to know?
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

It wasn’t exactly the "magical, glowing" moment you see in the movies. When I saw those two lines, my first thought wasn't a rush of joy; it was an immediate, sharp calculation. I went straight to the calendar, counting weeks, assessing my age, and feeling the weight of the previous three births. Being thirty-nine, you just... you know the stats. You know the risks. I didn’t feel like a mother-to-be; I felt like a ticking clock.

The weeks that followed were suffocatingly clinical. I found myself obsessing over the developmental milestones, almost like I was studying for a board exam rather than preparing to bring a life into the world. I’d be at work, surrounded by sick kids, and my mind would wander to my own kick counts. I tried to intellectualize the fear—if I knew everything that *could* go wrong, maybe I could catch it before it did?

But honestly? It was exhausting. I spent a lot of time hiding in the bathroom at work, just trying to breathe through the waves of dread. I wanted to be excited, but I was so busy managing the trauma of the last time that I barely had space to feel anything else. It felt like walking on a tightrope, and I was just waiting for a gust of wind.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

You know, I expected to feel the same surge of protective love I felt with my first, but it was just… tempered. The dominant emotion was a kind of guarded vigilance. It surprised me how much I felt like I had to "hold" the pregnancy myself, almost like if I relaxed for a second, I’d lose the thread.

I think the biggest surprise was the anger. I felt angry at my own body for being older, and angry at the healthcare system that I’m so deeply a part of. Because I’m a nurse, I have this weird, bitter realization that I’m "in the club," but it doesn’t actually grant me safety. If anything, it makes me more cynical. 

I’d catch myself staring at my reflection in the breakroom mirror, looking for signs of fatigue, and I’d feel this disconnect. I’m thirty-nine, I’m a professional, I’m a mom of three, and yet I felt like a scared girl waiting for someone to tell me I was going to be okay. I didn't expect to feel so small. I think I wanted to be "the expert" who had it all under control, but instead, I was just a woman terrified of losing her agency again. It’s a lonely place to be when you’re supposed to be the one who knows better.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

Antenatal care for me is a bit of a tightrope walk. Every time I walk into that clinic, I’m checking to see if they’re listening to *me* or just reading the chart. 

I’ve had moments where the care felt routine—which, for a "low risk" patient, is supposed to be comforting. But when I’m in that stirrup seat, I don’t feel like a colleague. I feel like a subject. There’s a specific kind of frustration when a provider glosses over a concern because my stats look good. Sometimes, I’ll drop a medical term into the conversation—maybe something about my blood pressure or a lab value—just to force them to switch gears and talk to me as a peer. It’s a defense mechanism, I know that, but it’s the only way I feel like I might actually be heard.

There have been a few nurses who really *got* it. They didn't rush the physical exam, and they asked me how I was doing *outside* of the physiological stuff. Those moments were the only times I felt like I could unclench my jaw. But mostly, it’s a dance of keeping my guard up. I’m constantly evaluating their bedside manner, wondering if, when things get intense in the delivery room, they’ll see me as an equal or just someone who needs to be managed and moved along. It’s exhausting to always be analyzing the care you’re receiving while you’re trying to receive it.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

The screening process... that’s where the "nurse" side of my brain goes into overdrive. Because I know the testing landscape inside and out—the NIPT, the anatomy scans, the markers for this or that—it almost felt like a burden. I wasn't just a patient; I was an analyst of my own biology.

When it came to making decisions, I didn’t just "accept" what was offered. I scrutinized it. I spent hours reading journals, not because I didn't trust my providers, but because I didn't trust the *process*. I felt like if I could decode the data perfectly, I could somehow guarantee a "normal" outcome. That’s the lie we tell ourselves, isn’t it? That more information equals more control.

There was a moment during an early screening where the technician was silent for a few seconds too long. My heart stopped. I knew exactly what she was looking for, and I knew what the silence could mean. I had to physically grip the edge of the table to keep from grabbing the screen and pointing out what I was seeing myself. That’s the tragedy of being a nurse in this position: I’m never just experiencing the moment, I’m always evaluating the "what if." Deciding on screenings wasn't about "peace of mind"—it was about trying to outrun the anxiety. It was an intellectual exercise, but my heart was always terrified.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

That’s the thing—I’m surrounded by information all day. My bookshelves at work are lined with clinical textbooks on neonatology and maternal-fetal medicine. But those books are sterile; they don't capture the crushing weight of sitting in your nursery at 2:00 AM wondering if a specific movement felt "right."

Honestly, I mostly turned to my own intuition, even though I spent the whole time trying to suppress it. I found very little comfort in standard pregnancy apps; they felt patronizing, filled with cutesy milestones that didn't account for someone with my level of medical literacy. I didn't want a "baby-sized fruit" comparison; I wanted to know about placental perfusion and statistical outcomes.

My real support? It was quiet. It was my husband, who let me vent when the "nurse" persona slipped. And, strangely, it was the small, guarded conversations I’d have with other moms who had been through trauma—not the ones who breezed through, but the ones who knew what it felt like to have the rug pulled out from under them. I couldn't lean on my professional peers, because I didn't want them to see me as vulnerable or "anxious." I had to maintain that shield. So, I mostly lived in my head, constantly fact-checking my own fears. It’s a lonely way to grow a human.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

Physically, it’s a constant negotiation. At thirty-nine, my body doesn't bounce back or carry the extra weight quite like it did ten years ago. My back aches in ways that remind me of my long shifts, and there’s a persistent fatigue that’s deeper than just being tired. I’ve spent my career helping patients manage their physical limitations, but feeling my own body shifting, stretching, and becoming heavy? It’s surreal. I feel like an observer in my own skin, watching these changes with a mix of awe and, if I’m being honest, a bit of betrayal.

Emotionally, the body changes are linked to my fear. Every kick, every Braxton Hicks contraction, is a data point I’m analyzing. I feel the baby move and I immediately check the "rhythm"—is it normal? Is it too much? Not enough? It’s hard to just be *in* my body when I’m constantly treating it like a patient I need to monitor. I find myself looking in the mirror and not recognizing the person staring back—I see a mother, yes, but I also see all the "risk factors" assigned to my age. It makes me feel less like a person and more like a vessel that needs constant guarding. I’m trying to embrace it, but it’s hard to love the process when you're so busy bracing for the impact.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

The hardest part isn’t the physical exhaustion or the way my clothes don't fit; it’s the loss of autonomy. As a nurse, my hands are my tools—I use them to help, to heal, to perform procedures. During this pregnancy, my body feels like it’s been co-opted. I look at my hands now, swollen and a bit puffy from the fluid retention, and they don’t feel like the hands of the clinician who can fix things. They feel like the hands of someone who is being acted *upon*.

There’s a vulnerability in that surrender that I really struggle with. Being thirty-nine, I’m acutely aware of the "aging" label attached to my pregnancy. My body feels more fragile, and every time I have to ask for help—whether it’s reaching for something or catching my breath—it’s a reminder that I’m not the "invincible" nurse right now. I’m the patient. I think that’s the bit that really messes with my head: acknowledging that I can’t "nursing" my way out of the changes my body is going through. I have to just let it happen, and that feels like the ultimate loss of control. It’s hard to reconcile the person I am at the hospital with the person I am at home, blooming and swelling and needing care myself.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

My husband is a saint, but I know I’m not always an easy person to be around right now. He’s the one who sees the mask slip when I finally close the door at night. He tries to be the "rational" one, reminding me that the baby is kicking, that the tests were clear, and that I’m healthy. I know he’s trying to ground me, but sometimes his calm makes me feel even more isolated because it highlights just how deep my own panic runs. 

When it comes to my family and friends, I tend to pull back. I have this "super-mom" reputation—the nurse who handles everything—and I’m terrified of letting that image crack. If I tell them I’m scared, they might start treating me like I’m fragile, and I don't want the pity. I don't want the "oh, you’re just a nervous pregnant lady" talk. 

So, I keep them at a distance. I give them the curated version of the pregnancy. It’s isolating, honestly. I’m surrounded by people who love me, but they only see the surface. I feel like I’m holding this heavy secret of anxiety all by myself because I’m too proud—or maybe too protective of my professional identity—to show them the version of me that’s actually shivering inside.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

The fears aren't just one thing; they’re a constant, low-frequency hum. It’s the silence of the fetal heart monitor that terrifies me, even when I know the rhythm is perfectly textbook. As a nurse, I’ve seen the "one in a thousand" outcomes. You spend your life trying to provide care, but you also see the moments where care just isn't enough, where tragedy happens despite everyone doing everything right. That’s the seed of my anxiety.

I’m terrified of losing my voice in the delivery room. I have this recurring nightmare—not of the pain, but of the environment. I see myself back in that cold, fluorescent room, surrounded by people who are looking at my vitals instead of my face. I worry that if I raise a concern, I’ll be dismissed as a "difficult" patient because of my medical background. There’s that fine, invisible line between being an informed patient and an annoying one, and I’m scared that in the heat of labor, I’ll be pushed to the latter.

And for the baby? I worry about everything from cord accidents to undetected complications. I intellectualize it, I read the charts, I check the kicks, but deep down? I’m just a mother who is terrified that her expertise will become a barrier to the basic human connection I need to get through this safely.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

Planning for this birth has been a paradoxical experience. I’ve written and rewritten a birth plan a dozen times, but I keep ripping it up because, deep down, I know that plans are just fictions we write to feel safe. Being a nurse, I know how fast a "textbook" delivery can turn into an emergency. I’ve been the one holding the crash cart; I know exactly how quickly the atmosphere in a room shifts from peaceful to chaotic.

I’m striving for a "quiet" birth—not necessarily a silent one, but a space where my autonomy is respected. I’ve tried to articulate what I need in writing: that I want to be spoken to as a partner in my care, not a subordinate. I’ve asked for delayed interventions where possible, and I’ve been very clear about my need for someone—anyone—to actually look me in the eye when they talk to me. 

But planning feels like a desperate attempt to set boundaries around a space where I’ve previously felt violated. I don’t just want a healthy baby; I need to come out the other side feeling like I still own my own body. That’s the real goal. I’m preparing by trying to find the words to advocate for myself without sounding like I’m "trouble," hoping that this time, my expertise will be treated as an asset rather than a complication.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

The birth... it was a blur of fluorescent lights and that familiar, clinical hum that I’ve spent my life surrounded by. When I finally arrived, it felt like the walls of the room were closing in, exactly the way I feared. Everything felt performative. Even in the throes of labor, I found myself correcting the terminology the resident was using, a reflex I couldn't stop. It was like I was back in the breakroom, trying to hold my own against people who viewed me as just another file on the chart.

When things started to move quickly, I felt that familiar, cold surge of panic—the feeling of being stripped of my agency. There was a moment where a monitor started alarming, and I saw that look—the one I’ve seen on my own colleagues' faces a hundred times—where they just *do* things to you without asking, because they assume you’re the patient and they’re the experts.

I wanted to scream, "I know what you're doing, I know why you're doing it, just tell me!" But I was too exhausted, too small. I felt invisible again. They were "managing" the delivery, and I was just the cargo. It wasn't the empowering, "present" experience I’d hoped for. It was a recovery from the environment itself, more than anything else. I got my baby, thank God, but the birth... that part is still something I’m trying to reconcile. It felt like I was back in a uniform, but this time, the patient was me.
At what moments during the birth did you feel most supported — and were there moments when you felt alone or unheard?

The moments I felt supported were almost painfully small. There was this one nurse—not the one in charge of the medical decisions, but a younger one who just held my hand through a particularly intense contraction. She didn’t try to explain the physiology to me, and she didn’t use medical shorthand. She just looked at me, saw the panic I was trying so hard to bury, and said, "You’re doing great, Flo. Just breathe." She didn't treat me like a colleague or a liability; she treated me like a mother. That was the only time the clinical coldness receded.

But the moments of feeling alone? They were everywhere else. Every time someone spoke *about* me to another provider while I was right there, I felt erased. There was a moment when they were discussing an intervention and they didn't even acknowledge my presence, even though they knew I was a nurse. I felt like a piece of furniture that was getting in the way of the procedure. 

I remember closing my eyes and trying to retreat into a headspace where I was just a person, not a set of clinical markers, but the beeping—the incessant, rhythmic, detached beeping of the machines—kept pulling me back into the system. I was surrounded by a dozen people, all experts, all "doing their jobs," yet I have never felt more profoundly alone in my entire life.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

The conversation around pain management felt… clinical. It always does. Because of my background, they didn't treat me like a woman experiencing pain; they treated me like a patient making a decision on a menu. They threw terms like "epidural rate," "contraindications," and "pharmacological support" at me as if that made the agony any easier to process. 

I chose to delay, partly because I didn't want to lose that last shred of connection to my body, even if my body felt like it was tearing apart. But the moment the pain became more than just a physiological sensation—when it started to become traumatic, when I could feel the panic rising in my throat—I asked for help. And I felt such… shame. I know it shouldn’t be a failing to ask for pain relief, but there’s this unspoken pressure on nurses to "push through" or "manage" our own thresholds. 

When the epidural finally happened, I felt that familiar relief, but it was bittersweet. It was like I was checking a box on a chart. The physical pain quieted down, sure, but the emotional ache of being *out of control* intensified. I wasn't just managing the pain; I was being sedated by the system. It felt like I was being removed from the process of my own birth, and that was the hardest pill to swallow. I wanted to be "the strong one," but I just wanted it to stop.
Were there options you wanted that weren't available, or options you were steered towards that you weren't sure about?

There’s this unspoken script in the delivery room, and if you’re a nurse, they expect you to know your lines. I wanted a low-intervention birth—not because I'm naive about the risks, but because I wanted to feel *present*. I wanted to move, to be upright, to feel the physical work of bringing my baby into the world. But every time I suggested something that veered off the standard protocol, I was met with that polite, condescending "doctor's voice." They’d explain the "risks" back to me, treating me like I didn't already have that data memorized.

I felt steered toward the bed, toward the continuous fetal monitoring, and toward the standard labor progression "timeline" that every hospital follows. They wanted me to be a "good patient" who fits the expected curve. When I questioned the constant monitoring—which made me feel tethered and anxious—I was treated like I was being difficult or unreasonable, as if my professional skepticism was a personal attack on their practice. 

I wasn't looking for a "natural" birth as some kind of badge of honor; I just wanted to be a human being, not a data point in a clinical pathway. I ended up feeling steered into a corner where the only "safe" option was the one that made me feel the most silenced. It’s hard to advocate for your own agency when the entire system is built to make you comply for the sake of efficiency.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

My husband was there, trying to be the bridge between me and the clinical team, but he was drowning, too. He kept looking at the monitors, trusting the doctors because he figured, "They’re the experts, they know what they’re doing." Every time he reassured me that "the team has it under control," it felt like a knife. He didn't realize that my fear wasn't of the *clinical outcome*—I trusted their skills, really—my fear was of the *process*. 

The doctors and nurses? Their presence was efficient, but it felt hollow. They were professional, yes, but there was a distance between us that felt insurmountable. They were "doing their jobs," performing the maneuvers, checking the cervical dilation, calling out the heart rates. But to them, I was just a professional colleague who had temporarily swapped roles. There was this underlying expectation that because I was a nurse, I shouldn't need emotional support, or that I’d be "fine" with the routine, cold nature of the care. 

I felt like an object being studied. Even the midwife, who I thought would be the one person to bridge that gap, felt like she was reading from a script. I wanted someone to hold space for the *human* experiencing the birth, not the "gravida 4" patient. I felt profoundly alone in a room full of people who were technically looking right at me, but seeing right through me.
Was there a particular person — whether a professional or someone close to you — whose presence made a real difference? What did they do?

There was one nurse, an older woman who came in during a shift change when I was really spiraling. I think she had been a labor nurse for a long time—she had that quiet, unhurried energy that you can’t fake. She didn’t look at the monitors first; she looked at my face. She saw that I wasn't just in labor—I was in the middle of a panic attack about the clinical environment itself.

She didn't try to "fix" me with medical jargon or remind me of the statistics. Instead, she just pulled up a stool, sat right next to my bed, and put her hand on my shoulder—not checking for a pulse, just making contact. She leaned in close and whispered, "I know you know all the equipment in this room, but for the next hour, I need you to forget it. You’re not a nurse right now. You’re just a mom."

That one sentence shattered the wall I’d been building. It allowed me to stop being the "colleague" and finally let myself be the patient. She didn't move any faster or do anything technically different than the others, but she gave me permission to stop being responsible for my own care. That small, human moment of acknowledgment made all the difference. It was the only time in that entire room that I felt like I existed.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

The birth ended with a frantic sort of relief. It wasn't the "empowering, transformational" scene you see in those books I used to keep on my desk. It was just... loud. There was that immediate flurry of activity, that sudden pivot to post-partum care, and for the first time in hours, the monitors weren't the main characters of the room. My baby was finally here, and the transition from "patient in crisis" to "mother holding her child" felt like a cliff.

In those first few minutes, I felt this strange, hollowed-out exhaustion. I was physically here, but I felt like I had spent the entire time trying to convince the room I was a human being, not a pathology. When they finally handed me my baby, I didn't feel like a champion. I felt like a survivor. 

I looked at the staff—the people who had just delivered my child—and I felt a weird mix of gratitude for their technical skill and this lingering, sharp sadness that they hadn't seen the person behind the scrubs. I felt relieved it was over, but I also felt a little bit broken. I hadn't lost my life, but I felt like I’d lost the battle for my own dignity. I just wanted everyone to leave so I could finally, truly, be a mother in peace.
If things didn't go to plan — or if there were interventions or complications — how were those explained or communicated to you at the time?

Communication was... abrupt. When the decision was made for an intervention, it wasn't a conversation; it was a clinical directive. There was no, "Flo, here's what we’re seeing, what do you think?" Instead, it was, "We’re going to do X because of Y." Even though I knew the clinical rationale behind the intervention, the *lack of inclusion* in that decision-making process was a slap in the face.

Because they knew I was a nurse, I think they assumed I’d just "get it." They’d fire off the rationale like they were briefing a colleague on a patient case, skipping over the part where I was the human experiencing the procedure. It felt like they were trying to bypass my anxiety by being overly clinical, but it just made me feel invisible. 

There was no space for the "why" that mattered to me. I didn't need a medical lecture; I needed to be treated with enough respect to be part of the loop. Being told what was happening to my own body felt like a loss of power, a sharp reminder that in that room, my badge and my history didn't matter. I was just a body to be managed, and they were the ones in charge. It wasn't explained to me; it was *inflicted* on me.
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

The room felt like an interrogation chamber, though I know objectively it was just a standard, modern labor and delivery suite. It was sterile, overly bright, and humming with the noise of machines that I, in my professional life, rely on to save lives. But in that moment, those machines felt like a cage. Every monitor, every IV pole, every tray of equipment was a reminder that I was being watched, monitored, and measured.

I wanted the room to feel like a sanctuary—somewhere I could safely transition into motherhood. But instead, it felt like an extension of the hospital floor I work on, just with the roles reversed. The lighting was harsh, the walls were painted a color meant to be "calming" but felt like clinical apathy, and there was absolutely no privacy. People walked in and out constantly—residents, nurses, techs—each one checking a screen, each one looking at the data, not at me. 

It wasn't the right place for me, not because the equipment wasn't necessary, but because the environment itself left no room for the human experience. It was built for efficiency, for managing risk, and for keeping things on a timeline. It left no room for a woman who was trying to reclaim her body. I felt like a patient on a chart, not a mother-to-be.
How much choice did you have about where you gave birth — and did you have access to the options you wanted?

The choice felt like an illusion. Being a nurse, I know the local hospital systems intimately—I know which units have the highest intervention rates, which ones are understaffed, and which ones prioritize "efficiency" over patient agency. My "choice" was essentially choosing which set of problems I was willing to live with. 

I really wanted a setting that felt less… industrialized. I craved a midwifery-led center, something where the medical machinery wasn't front and center, where I could just labor without the constant, rhythmic, mechanical assessment of every single contraction. But because of my age and the "gravida 4" label in my chart, I was steered toward the high-level maternity unit. They made it sound like a safety requirement, and while I couldn't argue with the clinical logic—I’m a nurse, after all—I felt like my autonomy was being traded for their liability management. 

There were no other real options that felt "safe" enough to my clinical brain, but that's the trap, isn't it? If you have enough knowledge to know what can go wrong, you’re almost forced to choose the place that creates the most rigid, clinical environment. I didn't feel like I had a menu of choices; I felt like I was being herded into a pen where the rules were already written.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I wanted a partnership. That’s the dream, right? I wanted them to see me—the woman, the mother, the person who happens to also know how to interpret a fetal heart rate strip. I imagined us standing on the same side of the bed, looking at the same goal: a healthy baby and a mother who felt respected. I wanted to be able to say, "I’m scared," and have them respond with kindness rather than just a clinical protocol for anxiety.

But I was terrified that my own background would get in the way. I worried they’d see my expertise as a threat to their authority. There’s this quiet, simmering tension between "the patient who knows too much" and the provider who wants to keep things simple. I was scared that if I asked a question about a medication or a procedure, they’d label me as "difficult." 

And, God, I was so afraid of being "the nurse patient." You know, the one who’s supposed to be "cool" and "low-maintenance." I worried that they’d rely on my knowledge to skip the emotional care I actually needed. I wanted them to realize that even though I know the science of birth, I’m still just as vulnerable as anyone else. I was terrified they’d overlook the human being because they were so busy checking off the boxes for the medical professional. That fear… it haunted every single appointment.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

The conversations felt less like a romantic decision and more like a board meeting. With my age staring us in the face—the dreaded "advanced maternal age" marker—our talks were saturated with clinical reality. I felt this intense pressure to make the "right" choice, as if one wrong move would seal our fate.

I think I carried the weight of the decision more than my husband did. I was the one who had to physically carry the pregnancy, the one who’d be subject to the tests, the one who’d be in that hospital bed. Yet, even with all my nursing knowledge, I felt this strange, quiet desperation to have him be the one to tell me it was going to be okay. He wanted this child so much, but he couldn't grasp the level of risk I was perpetually calculating. 

When we finally decided, it didn't feel like a leap of faith; it felt like a calculated risk. I felt like I had the most "say" because I was the one doing the math, but it didn't feel like a win. It felt like I was the only one holding the map, and I was terrified that if I chose the wrong route, the entire responsibility would be on my shoulders. It wasn't a partnership of equals; it was a partnership of one person trying to shield the other from the reality of the stakes.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before this pregnancy, I generally felt like my body was a reliable instrument. It had carried three children before, and in my professional life, I saw it as a sturdy, capable machine that could handle long shifts, physical labor, and the high-stakes environment of a hospital. I trusted it because it had never truly failed me.

But the second I started thinking about this one, the narrative shifted. The "advanced maternal age" tag in my own mind turned my body from a tool into a liability. It’s like the medical literature I’ve read my whole career suddenly turned its lens on me. I stopped seeing my body as something that could "do" things and started seeing it as something that could "break."

Those feelings of betrayal were immediate. I’d look in the mirror and think, *Are you still up to this? Can you hold another life without something going wrong?* It’s a bizarre, almost violent shift in self-perception. You go from feeling like the architect of your own physical reality to feeling like you’re waiting for a faulty part to fail. That loss of trust in my own biology was almost more exhausting than the physical symptoms of the pregnancy itself. I wasn't just worried about the baby; I was worried about the vessel.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

It’s funny you ask that. A few months before I got pregnant, I had a routine checkup with a GP. I remember sitting on the paper-covered table, and for a split second, I almost mentioned that we were considering trying for another. But I hesitated. I watched her scan my chart—my age, my history—and I saw that clinical "efficiency" in her eyes. It felt like she was already categorizing me into a risk bucket before I’d even opened my mouth.

That appointment was a preview of the maternity care to come. She was perfectly polite, efficient, and technically accurate, but there was no space for the "person" behind the data. When I asked a general question about prenatal wellness, she gave me a textbook, standard-of-care answer that didn't address the nuance of *my* life or *my* fears. 

It was a sterile, impersonal interaction that left me feeling like a file to be processed. I walked out of that office feeling a distinct sense of dread about the future. I knew then that the system wasn't built to hold the complexity of someone like me—someone who knows the science but is still human enough to be terrified. It felt like a warning that once I was officially "pregnant," the machine would take over, and I’d just be another name on a list to be managed.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

Before this, the conversations with my husband were constant but surprisingly surface-level. We’d talk about the "logistics"—our careers, the house, the cost of childcare, the impact on our other kids. We were incredibly practical. I think we were both terrified of the emotional weight, so we stuck to the spreadsheets. It was our way of creating a false sense of security.

With friends—especially those who are also nurses—the conversations were different. They were cynical, guarded, and laced with "war stories" from the hospital floor. When we talked about having another, there was this unspoken understanding of the dangers. We’d trade stats about late-term complications and the burnout rates in the unit. It sounds strange to say, but that felt more "real" than the excited, Hallmark-style conversations other people have. 

My husband’s input felt meaningful, but it was often frustrating because he didn't *live* in the medical reality like I did. He could just see the "result"—a baby—while I was constantly scanning for the "risks." The only truly meaningful conversations were the ones I had with a mentor of mine—a retired midwife who didn't give me advice, but just sat there and listened to me voice my fears about being "an older mother." She didn't try to dismiss the risks; she just acknowledged them. That validation was the only thing that actually made me feel seen.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

It was the number thirty-nine. That was the ghost in the room. Even before I was pregnant, the medical literature I’d studied for years felt like a countdown clock. I knew the complications, the increased risk of induction, the potential for complications that I’d seen play out in other women's lives. I looked in the mirror and felt like I was betraying my own body by asking it to do this again when it was, by clinical standards, "old."

I felt this strange tension: I knew my body was strong—it’s carried three children—but I also felt a deep sense of fragility. It’s like I had two selves. There was the professional, the nurse who knew the mechanics of birth, and then there was the woman who feared that being a mother at this age was an act of hubris. I felt like I was walking around with a giant, invisible asterisk next to my name. 

The uncertainty wasn't just about the biology; it was about my own identity. Could I handle the loss of control that comes with being a patient, especially when I’ve spent my career being the one *in* control? I was scared that if I got pregnant, I’d stop being "Flo the Nurse" and just become "the high-risk case." That felt like a massive loss of self before I even started.
Did you have any concerns about your weight, fitness, or a health condition that felt like it might be a barrier?

I’ve spent my career obsessing over BMI charts and health screenings for others, so when the lens turned on me, the irony was thick enough to choke on. I wasn't technically "unfit," but at thirty-nine, my recovery time—the way my knees would ache after a twelve-hour shift—made me acutely aware of my own aging. I kept wondering if I was "strong enough" to carry this, and by "strong," I didn't just mean muscle mass. I meant: could my body withstand the physical toll without failing?

I had this nagging, silent fear that if I didn't meet the "perfect" physical profile—which, let’s be honest, doesn't exist—I’d be flagged the moment I walked into the clinic. I was terrified of being lectured about my weight or my age before I’d even had a chance to show them who I was as a person. It felt like I was being pre-judged by the metrics, and I knew that if the providers started there, they’d never see past it.

It’s such a vulnerability, having your entire health status reduced to a few numbers on a page. I felt like I had to "perform" healthiness just to get a seat at the table without being treated like a broken machine. It’s an exhausting way to exist—always trying to stay ahead of the judgment you know is coming.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

Honestly, I struggled to find a single person who filled that role. Because of my nursing background, I felt a strange, self-imposed pressure to be the "source" for everyone else. When I had questions, I turned to journals and databases, but that only fed the anxiety. The professional relationships I had were fragmented; my own doctor was someone I saw as a peer, so I felt like I couldn't be vulnerable with them without losing my "professional" standing.

There was one retired OB/GYN I’d worked with years ago—a woman who carried a sense of quiet authority that didn't feel threatening. She was the closest thing I had to a "safe harbor." Whenever I felt like the weight of the "what-ifs" was becoming too much, I’d grab a coffee with her. She didn't offer me empty platitudes or tell me it would all be "magical." She just listened to the data I was stressing over and would say, "Flo, you’re looking at the numbers, but you’re not looking at the woman." 

She was the only one who could remind me that my identity as a professional didn't have to be at war with my identity as a mother-to-be. But even then, I only went to her when I was at my breaking point. I still held back a lot. I think I was terrified that if I leaned on anyone too hard, I’d collapse under the weight of it all.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

It’s a complicated question, because "free to choose" feels like such a luxury when you’re looking at a biological calendar. In my head, I knew exactly what the data said about my age and fertility, and that data acted like a relentless, invisible deadline. I didn't feel like I was leisurely deciding; I felt like I was being chased.

There was this subtle, pervasive pressure—from my own professional knowledge and from society’s expectations of a woman my age—that if I waited any longer, I’d be throwing away my "last chance." It wasn't a choice made in a vacuum; it was a decision made under duress, driven by the fear that if I didn't act *now*, the opportunity would simply vanish. 

I don't think it was ever fully *my* choice, not in the way I wanted it to be. It felt more like a negotiation with my own mortality. I was constantly calculating—if I start now, I can be back at work by then; if I wait, the risks go up by X percent. I wasn't listening to my heart; I was listening to the statistics. It felt like I was trying to "fit" a baby into the remaining space of my career and my age, and that never truly felt like a free, joyful decision. It felt like a race against time that I wasn't sure I could win.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

The financial piece is rarely spoken about in the quiet, but it’s always humming in the background. My husband and I both work full-time—he’s in a demanding role, and I’m a pediatric nurse—so we’re "comfortable" on paper, but we’re essentially funding a household of five, soon to be six. We live in a place where the cost of living feels like a constant, tightening pressure. I kept calculating: if I took an extended leave, what would that do to our mortgage? What would that do to our ability to put three, then four kids through school?

I felt this cultural expectation that I should be "established" enough to just slide this baby into a perfectly organized life. But life is never like that. I felt the timing was dictated by my career trajectory—the fear that if I stepped away for too long, I’d lose the seniority I’d worked decades to build. And yet, if I didn't step away, I was worried I’d be "that mother" who isn't present.

It wasn't ever "the right time." It was just "the time before it became impossible." I felt like I was being squeezed by the demands of a high-stress job, a high-cost area, and a biology that doesn't care about my bank account or my promotion schedule. It was never a matter of *wanting* to do it; it was a matter of feeling like I was being timed out by everything around me.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I started this pregnancy thinking I would be the pilot, armed with my knowledge and my "low risk" label. But the moment I sat down for that first prenatal visit, I realized the system is designed to be the pilot, not me.

Every time I was handed a stack of forms, a schedule of mandatory tests, or a pamphlet explaining the "standard of care," I felt the steering wheel slipping out of my hands. I’d walk in wanting a dialogue, but I was given a monologue. There were times, honestly, when I handed over control willingly—mostly because I was so exhausted by the "what ifs" that I just wanted to be told, "You're fine, just do this." It was a relief, in a twisted way, to stop being the one responsible for the safety of my own pregnancy.

But then there were the moments where I was forced to hand it over, and that was the hardest part. When a provider would dismiss a question as "just nerves," or when they’d pivot to a procedure without explaining it, I felt that agency being ripped away. I wasn't a partner anymore; I was a passenger in a vehicle I didn't get to steer, driven by people who were looking at the monitors instead of the person in the seat. It’s a terrible, helpless feeling to be in the middle of a process that is so profoundly *yours* and yet feel like you have no say in the destination.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

We weren't on the same page, not really. It was like we were reading two different books entirely. I was buried in the clinical data, scanning for red flags and preparing for the worst-case scenarios, while my husband was looking for the "normal" experience. He wanted to trust the system, and frankly, I think he needed me to trust it too, so he wouldn't have to carry the weight of my anxiety.

When it came time to make decisions—like the anatomy scan or the testing schedule—he would listen to the provider and say, "They know what they’re doing, Flo. Why are you questioning them?" And I’d feel this surge of frustration because he wasn't hearing the *subtext* of the medical advice. To him, the doctor was a source of truth; to me, the doctor was someone who had to be managed and negotiated with so I wouldn't lose my agency.

It created a massive wedge. I felt isolated because I couldn’t explain the fear without sounding like I was spiraling, and he felt helpless because he couldn't "fix" the way I was feeling. We’d leave the office and he’d try to talk about the baby’s name or the nursery, and I’d be mentally rehashing a comment the OB made about my age. We weren't a team; we were two people experiencing the same event from opposite ends of a very long, very lonely telescope.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

There were moments where I desperately needed him to be my anchor, especially when the anxiety flared. If he just listened—really listened—without trying to "solve" my fear by quoting the doctor’s reassurances, that made all the difference. When we were debating the logistics of our lives after the baby arrived—like the return to work or how we’d balance the chaos of four kids—I needed his input. That was *our* life, and I needed to know we were in that struggle together.

But when it came to the medical decisions? That felt like my burden alone. I knew the stakes, I knew the complications, and I felt like I was the one who had to navigate the clinical environment. If he disagreed with me, I felt like he was dismissing my expertise—the only thing I had to protect myself. I remember one appointment where he wanted to just agree to a procedure the doctor suggested, and I felt this sharp, hot resentment. It wasn't his body, and it wasn't his past trauma being triggered by the coldness of that exam room.

In those moments, I needed his *support*, not his opinion. I needed him to stand in the room and be a witness to what was being done to me, not a second voice reinforcing the medical system. I didn't want him to play doctor; I wanted him to be my advocate, even if he didn't fully understand why I was so scared. It was a fine line, but one we crossed constantly.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the thing that sticks with me most is how much I wanted—and still want—for people to understand that being an expert doesn’t make you invincible. There’s this strange, silent burden of being a nurse who is also a patient. You’re expected to have this "insider knowledge," but that knowledge is exactly what makes the clinical coldness so much harder to swallow. I’m not just seeing a doctor; I’m seeing a colleague, and I’m judging their bedside manner by the standards I try to hold myself to every single shift. 

It feels like there’s this wall of professionalism that prevents me from being seen as a human. I wish the system had room for that—for the person who knows the protocols but is still terrified. If there’s one thing I’d want the people in charge of maternity care to know, it’s that we aren't just "gravida" counts or risk profiles. We are whole people, carrying our own histories, our own trauma, and our own specific fears into that room. 

I just wanted to be spoken to, not spoken *at*. I wanted someone to look at me, not the chart. It sounds so simple, doesn't it? But it felt like the hardest thing in the world to ask for. Thank you for listening to the parts I usually hide. It’s been… heavy, but necessary.
